arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal, namely the user reply, tool output, terminal or GUI state change that follows each action, yet no existing agentic RL system recovers it as a live, online learning source. We present OpenClaw-RL, a framework built on a simple observation: next-state signals are universal, and policy can learn from all of them simultaneously."

# Search for hubotter, kleinebuening concurrent papers
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "hubotter reinforcement learning execution feedback distillation LLM",
      "shenfeld self-play on-policy distillation language model",
      "aligning language model next-state online policy improvement",
      "conversational agent personalization RL individual user preference adaptation",
      "personal AI agent private on-device fine-tuning user feedback"
    ],
    "max_results": 15,

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal, namely the user reply, tool output, terminal or GUI state change that follows each action, yet no existing agentic RL system recovers it as a live, online learning source. We present OpenClaw-RL, a framework built on a simple observation: next-state signals are universal, and policy can learn from all of them simultaneously."

# Search for hubotter, kleinebuening concurrent papers
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "hubotter reinforcement learning execution feedback distillation LLM",
      "shenfeld self-play on-policy distillation language model",
      "aligning language model next-state online policy improvement",
      "conversational agent personalization RL individual user preference adaptation",
      "personal AI agent private on-device fine-tuning user feedback"
    ],
    "max_results": 15,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -200
raw_arguments.description: Search for concurrent works and personalization papers

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal, namely the user reply, tool output, terminal or GUI state change that follows each action, yet no existing agentic RL system recovers it as a live, online learning source."

# Search for more specific papers on "better than your teacher" and concurrent 2026 work
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "online policy improvement conversational feedback next state signal 2025 2026",
      "LEAP LLM agent privileged state iterative fine-tuning execution",
      "OpenRLHF veRL slime framework asynchronous rollout training",
      "DemyAgent demystifying reinforcement learning agentic CURE",
      "process-supervised RL multimodal tool-use agent interactive"
    ],
    "max_results": 15,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -200
arguments.description: Search for LEAP, OpenRLHF, DemyAgent papers

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal, namely the user reply, tool output, terminal or GUI state change that follows each action, yet no existing agentic RL system recovers it as a live, online learning source."

# Search for more specific papers on "better than your teacher" and concurrent 2026 work
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "online policy improvement conversational feedback next state signal 2025 2026",
      "LEAP LLM agent privileged state iterative fine-tuning execution",
      "OpenRLHF veRL slime framework asynchronous rollout training",
      "DemyAgent demystifying reinforcement learning agentic CURE",
      "process-supervised RL multimodal tool-use agent interactive"
    ],
    "max_results": 15,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -200
raw_arguments.description: Search for LEAP, OpenRLHF, DemyAgent papers

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Get the full abstracts for key papers I need to examine more
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2509.14480"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'

echo "---RAGEN---"
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2504.20073"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'

echo "---AGENT LIGHTNING---"
# Already have this, now look for the "From Correction to Mastery" paper
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2509.14257"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'
arguments.description: Get abstracts for process-supervised RL, RAGEN, and correction papers

results.content:
"title": "Process-Supervised Reinforcement Learning for Interactive Multimodal Tool-Use Agents",
        "abstract": "Effective interactive tool use requires agents to master Tool Integrated Reasoning (TIR): a complex process involving multi-turn planning and long-context dialogue management. To train agents for this dynamic process, particularly in multi-modal contexts, we introduce a sandbox environment for reinforcement learning (RL) that supports interleaved speech-text rollouts. Our core strategy, Turn-level Adjudicated Reinforcement Learning (TARL), addresses the challenge of credit assignment in long-horizon tasks by employing a Large Language Model (LLM) as a judge to provide turn-level evaluation. To enhance exploration, we integrate a mixed-task training curriculum with mathematical reasoning problems. This unified approach boosts the task pass rate on the text-based $\u03c4$-bench by over 6% compared to strong RL baselines. Crucially, we demonstrate our framework's suitability for fine-tuning a multi-modal foundation model for agentic tasks. By training a base multi-modal LLM on interleaved speech-text rollouts, we equip it with tool-use abilities, paving the way for more natural, voice-driven interactive agents.",
---RAGEN---
        "title": "RAGEN: Understanding Self-Evolution in LLM Agents via Multi-Turn Reinforcement Learning",
        "abstract": "Training large language models (LLMs) as interactive agents presents unique challenges including long-horizon decision making and interacting with stochastic environment feedback. While reinforcement learning (RL) has enabled progress in static tasks, multi-turn agent RL training remains underexplored. We propose StarPO (State-Thinking-Actions-Reward Policy Optimization), a general framework for trajectory-level agent RL, and introduce RAGEN, a modular system for training and evaluating LLM agents. Our study on four stylized environments reveals three core findings. First, our agent RL training shows a recurring mode of Echo Trap where reward variance cliffs and gradient spikes; we address this with StarPO-S, a stabilized variant with trajectory filtering, critic incorporation, and gradient stabilization. Second, we find the shaping of RL rollouts would benefit from diverse initial states, medium interaction granularity and more frequent sampling. Third, we show that without fine-grained, reasoning-aware reward signals, agent reasoning hardly emerge through multi-turn RL and they may show shallow strategies or hallucinated thoughts. Code and environments are available at https://github.com/RAGEN-AI/RAGEN.",
---AGENT LIGHTNING---
        "title": "From Correction to Mastery: Reinforced Distillation of Large Language Model Agents",
        "abstract": "Large Language Model agents excel at solving complex tasks through iterative reasoning and tool use, but typically depend on ultra-large, costly backbones. Existing distillation approaches train smaller students to imitate full teacher trajectories, yet reasoning and knowledge gaps between the teacher and student can cause compounding errors. We propose SCoRe, a student-centered framework in which the student generates training trajectories and the teacher corrects only the earliest error, producing training data matched to the student's ability and exposing specific weaknesses. The student is first fine-tuned on corrected trajectories. Subsequently, short-horizon reinforcement learning starts from the verified prefix preceding the earliest error, with target rewards assigned at that step. This design encourages autonomous problem-solving beyond imitation and enhances training stability. On 12 challenging benchmarks, a 7B-parameter student distilled with SCoRe matches the agentic performance of a 72B-parameter teacher.",

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Get the full abstracts for key papers I need to examine more
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2509.14480"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'

echo "---RAGEN---"
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2504.20073"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'

echo "---AGENT LIGHTNING---"
# Already have this, now look for the "From Correction to Mastery" paper
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2509.14257"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'
raw_arguments.description: Get abstracts for process-supervised RL, RAGEN, and correction papers

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL. Next-state signals encode evaluative signals extracted as scalar rewards via a PRM judge, and directive signals recovered through Hindsight-Guided On-Policy Distillation."

# Search for specific concurrent work and Self-Rewarding LM
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "self-rewarding language model iterative DPO LLM-as-judge",
      "AIR AReal mei real-world agent reinforcement learning deployment",
      "hindsight experience replay language goal relabeling RL",
      "OpenRLHF scalable RLHF training decoupled actor critic",
      "multi-environment unified agent training terminal GUI code web"
    ],
    "max_results": 15,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -200
arguments.description: Search for self-rewarding LM, OpenRLHF, multi-env unified training

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL. Next-state signals encode evaluative signals extracted as scalar rewards via a PRM judge, and directive signals recovered through Hindsight-Guided On-Policy Distillation."

# Search for specific concurrent work and Self-Rewarding LM
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "self-rewarding language model iterative DPO LLM-as-judge",
      "AIR AReal mei real-world agent reinforcement learning deployment",
      "hindsight experience replay language goal relabeling RL",
      "OpenRLHF scalable RLHF training decoupled actor critic",
      "multi-environment unified agent training terminal GUI code web"
    ],
    "max_results": 15,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -200
raw_arguments.description: Search for self-rewarding LM, OpenRLHF, multi-env unified training

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Look into key papers from references: Buffer of Thoughts, SuperCorrect, ArCHer, HIR
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2602.04942"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'
echo "---"
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2502.10325"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'
echo "---"
# Find ArCHer paper
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/search" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"query": "ArCHer training language model agents hierarchical reward", "max_results": 5, "sort_by": "importance"}' \
  | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"' | head -30
arguments.description: Look up Privileged Information Distillation, PRM for Agents, ArCHer papers

results.content:
"title": "Privileged Information Distillation for Language Models",
        "abstract": "Training-time privileged information (PI) can enable language models to succeed on tasks they would otherwise fail, making it a powerful tool for reinforcement learning in hard, long-horizon settings. However, transferring capabilities learned with PI to policies that must act without it at inference time remains a fundamental challenge. We study this problem in the context of distilling frontier models for multi-turn agentic environments, which typically hide their internal reasoning and expose only action trajectories. This breaks standard distillation pipelines, since successful behavior is observable, but the reasoning process is not. For this, we introduce \u03c0-Distill, a joint teacher-student objective that trains a PI-conditioned teacher and an unconditioned student simultaneously using the same model. Additionally, we also introduce On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD), an alternative approach that trains using Reinforcement Learning (RL) with a reverse KL-penalty between the student and the PI-conditioned teacher. We show that both of these algorithms effectively distill frontier agents using action-only PI. Specifically, we find that \u03c0-Distill and, in some cases, OPSD, outperform industry standard practices (Supervised finetuning followed by RL) that assume access to full Chain-of-Thought supervision across multiple agentic benchmarks, models, and forms of PI. We complement our results with extensive analysis that characterizes the factors enabling effective learning with PI, focusing primarily on \u03c0-Distill and characterizing when OPSD is competitive.",
---
        "title": "Process Reward Models for LLM Agents: Practical Framework and Directions",
        "abstract": "We introduce Agent Process Reward Models (AgentPRM), a simple and scalable framework for training LLM agents to continually improve through interactions. AgentPRM follows a lightweight actor-critic paradigm, using Monte Carlo rollouts to compute reward targets and optimize policies. It requires minimal modifications to existing RLHF pipelines, making it easy to integrate at scale. Beyond AgentPRM, we propose InversePRM, which learns process rewards directly from demonstrations without explicit outcome supervision. We also explore key challenges and opportunities, including exploration, process reward shaping, and model-predictive reasoning. We evaluate on ALFWorld benchmark, show that small 3B models trained with AgentPRM and InversePRM outperform strong GPT-4o baselines, and analyze test-time scaling, reward hacking, and more. Our code is available at: https://github.com/sanjibanc/agent_prm.",
---
            "arxiv_id": "2401.10020",
            "title": "Self-Rewarding Language Models",
            "year": 2025,
            "arxiv_id": "2303.11366",
            "title": "Reflexion: Language Agents with Verbal Reinforcement Learning",
            "year": 2023,
            "arxiv_id": "2406.17744",
            "title": "Following Length Constraints in Instructions",
            "year": 2024,
            "arxiv_id": "2409.06927",
            "title": "Representation Tuning",
            "year": 2024,
            "arxiv_id": "2510.01925",
            "title": "Enhancing Large Language Model Reasoning with Reward Models: An Analytical Survey",
            "year": 2025,

This breaks standard distillation pipelines, since successful behavior is observable, but the reasoning process is not. For this, we introduce \u03c0-Distill, a joint teacher-student objective that trains a PI-conditioned teacher and an unconditioned student simultaneously using the same model. Additionally, we also introduce On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD), an alternative approach that trains using Reinforcement Learning (RL) with a reverse KL-penalty between the student and the PI-conditioned teacher. We show that both of these algorithms effectively distill frontier agents using action-only PI. Specifically, we find that \u03c0-Distill and, in some cases, OPSD, outperform industry standard practices (Supervised finetuning followed by RL) that assume access to full Chain-of-Thought supervision across multiple agentic benchmarks, models, and forms of PI. We complement our results with extensive analysis that characterizes the factors enabling effective learning with PI, focusing primarily on \u03c0-Distill and characterizing when OPSD is competitive.",
---
        "title": "Process Reward Models for LLM Agents: Practical Framework and Directions",
        "abstract": "We introduce Agent Process Reward Models (AgentPRM), a simple and scalable framework for training LLM agents to continually improve through interactions. AgentPRM follows a lightweight actor-critic paradigm, using Monte Carlo rollouts to compute reward targets and optimize policies. It requires minimal modifications to existing RLHF pipelines, making it easy to integrate at scale. Beyond AgentPRM, we propose InversePRM, which learns process rewards directly from demonstrations without explicit outcome supervision. We also explore key challenges and opportunities, including exploration, process reward shaping, and model-predictive reasoning. We evaluate on ALFWorld benchmark, show that small 3B models trained with AgentPRM and InversePRM outperform strong GPT-4o baselines, and analyze test-time scaling, reward hacking, and more. Our code is available at: https://github.com/sanjibanc/agent_prm.",

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Look into key papers from references: Buffer of Thoughts, SuperCorrect, ArCHer, HIR
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2602.04942"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'
echo "---"
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2502.10325"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'
echo "---"
# Find ArCHer paper
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/search" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"query": "ArCHer training language model agents hierarchical reward", "max_results": 5, "sort_by": "importance"}' \
  | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"' | head -30
raw_arguments.description: Look up Privileged Information Distillation, PRM for Agents, ArCHer papers

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL, a framework built on a simple observation: next-state signals are universal, and policy can learn from all of them simultaneously."

# Dedicated search for the Privileged Information Distillation paper (pi-distill/OPSD - very similar!)
# Also find hubotter 2026 and shenfeld 2026
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "pi-distill on-policy self-distillation privileged information agent multi-turn KL",
      "buffer thoughts thought templates retrieval augmented context LLM",
      "GRPO group relative policy optimization reward standardization",
      "DAPO decoupled policy optimization token-level loss",
      "process reward model step-level supervision math reasoning"
    ],
    "max_results": 15,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -150
arguments.description: Search for pi-distill OPSD, GRPO, DAPO, Buffer of Thoughts papers

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL, a framework built on a simple observation: next-state signals are universal, and policy can learn from all of them simultaneously."

# Dedicated search for the Privileged Information Distillation paper (pi-distill/OPSD - very similar!)
# Also find hubotter 2026 and shenfeld 2026
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "pi-distill on-policy self-distillation privileged information agent multi-turn KL",
      "buffer thoughts thought templates retrieval augmented context LLM",
      "GRPO group relative policy optimization reward standardization",
      "DAPO decoupled policy optimization token-level loss",
      "process reward model step-level supervision math reasoning"
    ],
    "max_results": 15,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -150
raw_arguments.description: Search for pi-distill OPSD, GRPO, DAPO, Buffer of Thoughts papers

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL, a framework built on a simple observation: next-state signals are universal, and policy can learn from all of them simultaneously."

# Find hubotter and kleinebuening papers directly
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "reinforced in-context span fine-tuning execution feedback 2025 2026",
      "aligning LLM agent next state online learning correction signal 2026",
      "RLVR reinforcement learning verifiable reward agent benchmark coding",
      "continual learning deployed agent live interactions improvement",
      "AReal real world agentic RL framework training infrastructure"
    ],
    "max_results": 15,
    "sort_by": "date",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -200
arguments.description: Search for hubotter-like and AReal infrastructure papers

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL, a framework built on a simple observation: next-state signals are universal, and policy can learn from all of them simultaneously."

# Find hubotter and kleinebuening papers directly
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "reinforced in-context span fine-tuning execution feedback 2025 2026",
      "aligning LLM agent next state online learning correction signal 2026",
      "RLVR reinforcement learning verifiable reward agent benchmark coding",
      "continual learning deployed agent live interactions improvement",
      "AReal real world agentic RL framework training infrastructure"
    ],
    "max_results": 15,
    "sort_by": "date",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -200
raw_arguments.description: Search for hubotter-like and AReal infrastructure papers

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Get the full abstract for "Just-In-Time RL" and "Internalizing Agency from Reflective Experience"
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2601.18510"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'
echo "---"
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2603.16843"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'
echo "---"
# Also check "OpenTinker" which might be relevant
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2601.07376"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'
arguments.description: Get abstracts for Just-In-Time RL, Internalizing Agency, OpenTinker papers

results.content:
"title": "Just-In-Time Reinforcement Learning: Continual Learning in LLM Agents Without Gradient Updates",
        "abstract": "While Large Language Model (LLM) agents excel at general tasks, they inherently struggle with continual adaptation due to the frozen weights after deployment. Conventional reinforcement learning (RL) offers a solution but incurs prohibitive computational costs and the risk of catastrophic forgetting. We introduce Just-In-Time Reinforcement Learning (JitRL), a training-free framework that enables test-time policy optimization without any gradient updates. JitRL maintains a dynamic, non-parametric memory of experiences and retrieves relevant trajectories to estimate action advantages on-the-fly. These estimates are then used to directly modulate the LLM's output logits. We theoretically prove that this additive update rule is the exact closed-form solution to the KL-constrained policy optimization objective. Extensive experiments on WebArena and Jericho demonstrate that JitRL establishes a new state-of-the-art among training-free methods. Crucially, JitRL outperforms the performance of computationally expensive fine-tuning methods (e.g., WebRL) while reducing monetary costs by over 30 times, offering a scalable path for continual learning agents. The code is available at https://github.com/liushiliushi/JitRL.",
---
        "title": "Internalizing Agency from Reflective Experience",
        "abstract": "Large language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents that must plan, act, and recover from mistakes through long-horizon interaction with environments that provide rich feedback. However, prevailing outcome-driven post-training methods (e.g., RL with verifiable rewards) primarily optimize final success signals, leaving rich environment feedback underutilized. Consequently, they often lead to distribution sharpening: the policy becomes better at reproducing a narrow set of already-successful behaviors, while failing to improve the feedback-grounded agency needed to expand problem-solving capacity (e.g., Pass@k) in long-horizon settings. To address this, we propose LEAFE (Learning Feedback-Grounded Agency from Reflective Experience), a framework that internalizes recovery agency from reflective experience. Specifically, during exploration, the agent summarizes environment feedback into actionable experience, backtracks to earlier decision points, and explores alternative branches with revised actions. We then distill these experience-guided corrections into the model through supervised fine-tuning, enabling the policy to recover more effectively in future interactions. Across a diverse set of interactive coding and agentic tasks under fixed interaction budgets, LEAFE consistently improves Pass@1 over the base model and achieves higher Pass@k than outcome-driven baselines (GRPO) and experience-based methods such as Early Experience, with gains of up to 14% on Pass@128.",
---
        "title": "OpenTinker: Separating Concerns in Agentic Reinforcement Learning",
        "abstract": "We introduce OpenTinker, an infrastructure for reinforcement learning (RL) of large language model (LLM) agents built around a separation of concerns across algorithm design, execution, and agent-environment interaction. Rather than relying on monolithic, end-to-end RL pipelines, OpenTinker decomposes agentic learning systems into lightweight, composable components with clearly defined abstraction boundaries. Users specify agents, environments, and interaction protocols, while inference and training are delegated to a managed execution runtime. OpenTinker introduces a centralized scheduler for managing training and inference workloads, including LoRA-based and full-parameter RL, supervised fine-tuning, and inference, over shared resources. We further discuss design principles for extending OpenTinker to multi-agent training. Finally, we present a set of RL use cases that demonstrate the effectiveness of the framework in practical agentic learning scenarios.",

raw_arguments.description: Extract OpenClaw-RL context and search for LEAFE paper

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal, namely the user reply, tool output, terminal or GUI state change that follows each action, yet no existing agentic RL system recovers it as a live, online learning source."

# Check the hubotter (reinforcement learning execution feedback) paper
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "execution feedback reinforcement fine-tuning LLM code math agent reward",
      "hindsight relabeling goal conditioned reward language model offline data",
      "wisdom hindsight instruction relabeling LLM goal conditioned reward"
    ],
    "max_results": 10,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -100
arguments.description: Search for specific referenced papers hubotter and zhang wisdom

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal, namely the user reply, tool output, terminal or GUI state change that follows each action, yet no existing agentic RL system recovers it as a live, online learning source."

# Check the hubotter (reinforcement learning execution feedback) paper
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "execution feedback reinforcement fine-tuning LLM code math agent reward",
      "hindsight relabeling goal conditioned reward language model offline data",
      "wisdom hindsight instruction relabeling LLM goal conditioned reward"
    ],
    "max_results": 10,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -100
raw_arguments.description: Search for specific referenced papers hubotter and zhang wisdom

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Web search for SOTA on OSWorld, SWE-Bench-Verified, and AIME benchmarks
tvly search "OSWorld benchmark SOTA results 2025 2026 GUI agent performance" --max-results 5 --depth advanced

echo "---"
tvly search "SWE-Bench verified SOTA results 2025 2026 best performance" --max-results 5
arguments.description: Search for SOTA on OSWorld and SWE-Bench benchmarks

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL, a framework built on a simple observation: next-state signals are universal, and policy can learn from all of them simultaneously."

# Find related papers for Privileged Information Distillation (pi-distill OPSD) 
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/find_related" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2602.04942", "max_results": 10}' \
  | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"' | head -50
arguments.description: Find papers related to Privileged Information Distillation

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL."

# Final targeted batch search looking for missing baselines, alternative framings
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "experience-based self-improving agent rollback correction fine-tune GRPO 2026",
      "online reinforcement learning agent user preference style personalization LLM",
      "TARL turn-level adjudicated RL agentic LLM credit assignment multimodal",
      "math shepherd process reward model automatic step supervision MC estimation",
      "RLHF live deployment serving inference training decoupled weight update"
    ],
    "max_results": 15,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -200
arguments.description: Final targeted search for missing baselines and alternative approaches

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL."

# Final targeted batch search looking for missing baselines, alternative framings
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "experience-based self-improving agent rollback correction fine-tune GRPO 2026",
      "online reinforcement learning agent user preference style personalization LLM",
      "TARL turn-level adjudicated RL agentic LLM credit assignment multimodal",
      "math shepherd process reward model automatic step supervision MC estimation",
      "RLHF live deployment serving inference training decoupled weight update"
    ],
    "max_results": 15,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -200
raw_arguments.description: Final targeted search for missing baselines and alternative approaches

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL."

# Search for the process-supervised RL for tool-use agents which seems very close
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2509.14480"}' | python3 -m json.tool

echo "---RLAnything abstract---"
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2602.02488"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'
arguments.description: Get detailed info on TARL process supervised RL and RLAnything papers

results.content:
{
    "paper": {
        "arxiv_id": "2509.14480",
        "title": "Process-Supervised Reinforcement Learning for Interactive Multimodal Tool-Use Agents",
        "abstract": "Effective interactive tool use requires agents to master Tool Integrated Reasoning (TIR): a complex process involving multi-turn planning and long-context dialogue management. To train agents for this dynamic process, particularly in multi-modal contexts, we introduce a sandbox environment for reinforcement learning (RL) that supports interleaved speech-text rollouts. Our core strategy, Turn-level Adjudicated Reinforcement Learning (TARL), addresses the challenge of credit assignment in long-horizon tasks by employing a Large Language Model (LLM) as a judge to provide turn-level evaluation. To enhance exploration, we integrate a mixed-task training curriculum with mathematical reasoning problems. This unified approach boosts the task pass rate on the text-based $\u03c4$-bench by over 6% compared to strong RL baselines. Crucially, we demonstrate our framework's suitability for fine-tuning a multi-modal foundation model for agentic tasks. By training a base multi-modal LLM on interleaved speech-text rollouts, we equip it with tool-use abilities, paving the way for more natural, voice-driven interactive agents.",
        "authors": [
            "Weiting Tan",
            "Xinghua Qu",
            "Ming Tu",
            "Meng Ge",
            "Andy T. Liu",
            "Philipp Koehn",
            "Lu Lu"
        ],
        "categories": [
            "cs.CL",
            "cs.AI",
            "cs.MA"
        ],
        "primary_category": "cs.CL",
        "published": "2025-09-17 00:00:00",
        "year": 2025,
        "similarity_score": null,
        "pdf_url": "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.14480",
        "abs_url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.14480",
        "doi": null,
        "journal_ref": null,
        "comment": null
    }
}
---RLAnything abstract---
        "title": "RLAnything: Forge Environment, Policy, and Reward Model in Completely Dynamic RL System",
        "abstract": "We propose RLAnything, a reinforcement learning framework that dynamically forges environment, policy, and reward models through closed-loop optimization, amplifying learning signals and strengthening the overall RL system for any LLM or agentic scenarios. Specifically, the policy is trained with integrated feedback from step-wise and outcome signals, while the reward model is jointly optimized via consistency feedback, which in turn further improves policy training. Moreover, our theory-motivated automatic environment adaptation improves training for both the reward and policy models by leveraging critic feedback from each, enabling learning from experience. Empirically, each added component consistently improves the overall system, and RLAnything yields substantial gains across various representative LLM and agentic tasks, boosting Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking by 9.1% on OSWorld and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct by 18.7% and 11.9% on AlfWorld and LiveBench, respectively. We also that optimized reward-model signals outperform outcomes that rely on human labels. Code: https://github.com/Gen-Verse/Open-AgentRL",

Empirically, each added component consistently improves the overall system, and RLAnything yields substantial gains across various representative LLM and agentic tasks, boosting Qwen3-VL-8B-Thinking by 9.1% on OSWorld and Qwen2.5-7B-Instruct by 18.7% and 11.9% on AlfWorld and LiveBench, respectively. We also that optimized reward-model signals outperform outcomes that rely on human labels. Code: https://github.com/Gen-Verse/Open-AgentRL",

raw_tool_result.content:
{
    "paper": {
        "arxiv_id": "2509.14480",
        "title": "Process-Supervised Reinforcement Learning for Interactive Multimodal Tool-Use Agents",
        "abstract": "Effective interactive tool use requires agents to master Tool Integrated Reasoning (TIR): a complex process involving multi-turn planning and long-context dialogue management. To train agents for this dynamic process, particularly in multi-modal contexts, we introduce a sandbox environment for reinforcement learning (RL) that supports interleaved speech-text rollouts. Our core strategy, Turn-level Adjudicated Reinforcement Learning (TARL), addresses the challenge of credit assignment in long-horizon tasks by employing a Large Language Model (LLM) as a judge to provide turn-level evaluation. To enhance exploration, we integrate a mixed-task training curriculum with mathematical reasoning problems. This unified approach boosts the task pass rate on the text-based $\u03c4$-bench by over 6% compared to strong RL baselines. Crucially, we demonstrate our framework's suitability for fine-tuning a multi-modal foundation model for agentic tasks. By training a base multi-modal LLM on interleaved speech-text rollouts, we equip it with tool-use abilities, paving the way for more natural, voice-driven interactive agents.",

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL."

# Search for the process-supervised RL for tool-use agents which seems very close
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2509.14480"}' | python3 -m json.tool

echo "---RLAnything abstract---"
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2602.02488"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'
raw_arguments.description: Get detailed info on TARL process supervised RL and RLAnything papers

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL."

# Search for TARL paper and Agent Lightning  - these are clear direct competitors
# Also look for Reflexion as it's highly related
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "reflexion verbal reinforcement learning language agent self-reflection feedback",
      "TULU RLHF pipeline instruction following user preference online",
      "generative PRM chain-of-thought verification step-wise process reward",
      "agent RL training framework arbitrary environment agnostic decoupled ANY",
      "LLM agent self-improvement deployed feedback signal reward automatic"
    ],
    "max_results": 15,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -100
arguments.description: Search for Reflexion, generative PRM, agent RL ANY framework papers

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL."

# Search for TARL paper and Agent Lightning  - these are clear direct competitors
# Also look for Reflexion as it's highly related
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "reflexion verbal reinforcement learning language agent self-reflection feedback",
      "TULU RLHF pipeline instruction following user preference online",
      "generative PRM chain-of-thought verification step-wise process reward",
      "agent RL training framework arbitrary environment agnostic decoupled ANY",
      "LLM agent self-improvement deployed feedback signal reward automatic"
    ],
    "max_results": 15,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -100
raw_arguments.description: Search for Reflexion, generative PRM, agent RL ANY framework papers

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Get the Agent Lightning paper's full abstract and key details
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2508.03680"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"|"year"|"citation_count"'

echo "---"
# Find the references of OpenClaw-RL to get the papers they cited
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/references" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2603.10165", "limit": 30}' \
  | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"' | head -100
arguments.description: Get Agent Lightning details and OpenClaw-RL references

results.content:
"title": "Agent Lightning: Train ANY AI Agents with Reinforcement Learning",
        "abstract": "We present Agent Lightning, a flexible and extensible framework that enables Reinforcement Learning (RL)-based training of Large Language Models (LLMs) for any AI agent. Unlike existing methods that tightly couple RL training with agent or rely on sequence concatenation with masking, Agent Lightning achieves complete decoupling between agent execution and training, allowing seamless integration with existing agents developed via diverse ways (e.g., using frameworks like LangChain, OpenAI Agents SDK, AutoGen, and building from scratch) with almost ZERO code modifications. By formulating agent execution as Markov decision process, we define an unified data interface and propose a hierarchical RL algorithm, LightningRL, which contains a credit assignment module, allowing us to decompose trajectories generated by ANY agents into training transition. This enables RL to handle complex interaction logic, such as multi-agent scenarios and dynamic workflows. For the system design, we introduce a Training-Agent Disaggregation architecture, and brings agent observability frameworks into agent runtime, providing a standardized agent finetuning interface. Experiments across text-to-SQL, retrieval-augmented generation, and math tool-use tasks demonstrate stable, continuous improvements, showcasing the framework's potential for real-world agent training and deployment.",
        "year": 2025,

raw_tool_result.content:
"title": "Agent Lightning: Train ANY AI Agents with Reinforcement Learning",
        "abstract": "We present Agent Lightning, a flexible and extensible framework that enables Reinforcement Learning (RL)-based training of Large Language Models (LLMs) for any AI agent. Unlike existing methods that tightly couple RL training with agent or rely on sequence concatenation with masking, Agent Lightning achieves complete decoupling between agent execution and training, allowing seamless integration with existing agents developed via diverse ways (e.g., using frameworks like LangChain, OpenAI Agents SDK, AutoGen, and building from scratch) with almost ZERO code modifications. By formulating agent execution as Markov decision process, we define an unified data interface and propose a hierarchical RL algorithm, LightningRL, which contains a credit assignment module, allowing us to decompose trajectories generated by ANY agents into training transition. This enables RL to handle complex interaction logic, such as multi-agent scenarios and dynamic workflows. For the system design, we introduce a Training-Agent Disaggregation architecture, and brings agent observability frameworks into agent runtime, providing a standardized agent finetuning interface. Experiments across text-to-SQL, retrieval-augmented generation, and math tool-use tasks demonstrate stable, continuous improvements, showcasing the framework's potential for real-world agent training and deployment.",
        "year": 2025,

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Get the Agent Lightning paper's full abstract and key details
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2508.03680"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"|"year"|"citation_count"'

echo "---"
# Find the references of OpenClaw-RL to get the papers they cited
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/references" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2603.10165", "limit": 30}' \
  | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"' | head -100
raw_arguments.description: Get Agent Lightning details and OpenClaw-RL references

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Get the RL via Self-Distillation paper (2601.20802) - very relevant
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2601.20802"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'

echo "---"
# Get the self-distillation enables continual learning paper (2601.19897)
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2601.19897"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'

echo "---"
# Get PRIME (Process Reinforcement through Implicit Rewards) abstract
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2502.01456"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'
arguments.description: Get RL via Self-Distillation, continual learning, and PRIME papers

results.content:
"title": "Reinforcement Learning via Self-Distillation",
        "abstract": "Large language models are increasingly post-trained with reinforcement learning in verifiable domains such as code and math. Yet, current methods for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) learn only from a scalar outcome reward per attempt, creating a severe credit-assignment bottleneck. Many verifiable environments actually provide rich textual feedback, such as runtime errors or judge evaluations, that explain why an attempt failed. We formalize this setting as reinforcement learning with rich feedback and introduce Self-Distillation Policy Optimization (SDPO), which converts tokenized feedback into a dense learning signal without any external teacher or explicit reward model. SDPO treats the current model conditioned on feedback as a self-teacher and distills its feedback-informed next-token predictions back into the policy. In this way, SDPO leverages the model's ability to retrospectively identify its own mistakes in-context. Across scientific reasoning, tool use, and competitive programming on LiveCodeBench v6, SDPO improves sample efficiency and final accuracy over strong RLVR baselines. Notably, SDPO also outperforms baselines in standard RLVR environments that only return scalar feedback by using successful rollouts as implicit feedback for failed attempts. Finally, applying SDPO to individual questions at test time accelerates discovery on difficult binary-reward tasks, achieving the same discovery probability as best-of-k sampling or multi-turn conversations with 3x fewer attempts.",
---
        "title": "Self-Distillation Enables Continual Learning",
        "abstract": "Continual learning, enabling models to acquire new skills and knowledge without degrading existing capabilities, remains a fundamental challenge for foundation models. While on-policy reinforcement learning can reduce forgetting, it requires explicit reward functions that are often unavailable. Learning from expert demonstrations, the primary alternative, is dominated by supervised fine-tuning (SFT), which is inherently off-policy. We introduce Self-Distillation Fine-Tuning (SDFT), a simple method that enables on-policy learning directly from demonstrations. SDFT leverages in-context learning by using a demonstration-conditioned model as its own teacher, generating on-policy training signals that preserve prior capabilities while acquiring new skills. Across skill learning and knowledge acquisition tasks, SDFT consistently outperforms SFT, achieving higher new-task accuracy while substantially reducing catastrophic forgetting. In sequential learning experiments, SDFT enables a single model to accumulate multiple skills over time without performance regression, establishing on-policy distillation as a practical path to continual learning from demonstrations.",
---
        "title": "Process Reinforcement through Implicit Rewards",
        "abstract": "Dense process rewards have proven a more effective alternative to the sparse outcome-level rewards in the inference-time scaling of large language models (LLMs), particularly in tasks requiring complex multi-step reasoning. While dense rewards also offer an appealing choice for the reinforcement learning (RL) of LLMs since their fine-grained rewards have the potential to address some inherent issues of outcome rewards, such as training efficiency and credit assignment, this potential remains largely unrealized. This can be primarily attributed to the challenges of training process reward models (PRMs) online, where collecting high-quality process labels is prohibitively expensive, making them particularly vulnerable to reward hacking. To address these challenges, we propose PRIME (Process Reinforcement through IMplicit rEwards), which enables online PRM updates using only policy rollouts and outcome labels through implict process rewards. PRIME combines well with various advantage functions and forgoes the dedicated reward model training phrase that existing approaches require, substantially reducing the development overhead. We demonstrate PRIME's effectiveness on competitional math and coding. Starting from Qwen2.5-Math-7B-Base, PRIME achieves a 15.1% average improvement across several key reasoning benchmarks over the SFT model. Notably, our resulting model, Eurus-2-7B-PRIME, surpasses Qwen2.5-Math-7B-Instruct on seven reasoning benchmarks with 10% of its training data.",

        "title": "Self-Distillation Enables Continual Learning",
        "abstract": "Continual learning, enabling models to acquire new skills and knowledge without degrading existing capabilities, remains a fundamental challenge for foundation models. While on-policy reinforcement learning can reduce forgetting, it requires explicit reward functions that are often unavailable. Learning from expert demonstrations, the primary alternative, is dominated by supervised fine-tuning (SFT), which is inherently off-policy. We introduce Self-Distillation Fine-Tuning (SDFT), a simple method that enables on-policy learning directly from demonstrations. SDFT leverages in-context learning by using a demonstration-conditioned model as its own teacher, generating on-policy training signals that preserve prior capabilities while acquiring new skills. Across skill learning and knowledge acquisition tasks, SDFT consistently outperforms SFT, achieving higher new-task accuracy while substantially reducing catastrophic forgetting. In sequential learning experiments, SDFT enables a single model to accumulate multiple skills over time without performance regression, establishing on-policy distillation as a practical path to continual learning from demonstrations.",
---
        "title": "Process Reinforcement through Implicit Rewards",
        "abstract": "Dense process rewards have proven a more effective alternative to the sparse outcome-level rewards in the inference-time scaling of large language models (LLMs), particularly in tasks requiring complex multi-step reasoning. While dense rewards also offer an appealing choice for the reinforcement learning (RL) of LLMs since their fine-grained rewards have the potential to address some inherent issues of outcome rewards, such as training efficiency and credit assignment, this potential remains largely unrealized. This can be primarily attributed to the challenges of training process reward models (PRMs) online, where collecting high-quality process labels is prohibitively expensive, making them particularly vulnerable to reward hacking. To address these challenges, we propose PRIME (Process Reinforcement through IMplicit rEwards), which enables online PRM updates using only policy rollouts and outcome labels through implict process rewards. PRIME combines well with various advantage functions and forgoes the dedicated reward model training phrase that existing approaches require, substantially reducing the development overhead. We demonstrate PRIME's effectiveness on competitional math and coding. Starting from Qwen2.5-Math-7B-Base, PRIME achieves a 15.1% average improvement across several key reasoning benchmarks over the SFT model. Notably, our resulting model, Eurus-2-7B-PRIME, surpasses Qwen2.5-Math-7B-Instruct on seven reasoning benchmarks with 10% of its training data.",
[metadata] {"noOutputExpected": false}

tool_use_result.stdout:
"title": "Reinforcement Learning via Self-Distillation",
        "abstract": "Large language models are increasingly post-trained with reinforcement learning in verifiable domains such as code and math. Yet, current methods for reinforcement learning with verifiable rewards (RLVR) learn only from a scalar outcome reward per attempt, creating a severe credit-assignment bottleneck. Many verifiable environments actually provide rich textual feedback, such as runtime errors or judge evaluations, that explain why an attempt failed. We formalize this setting as reinforcement learning with rich feedback and introduce Self-Distillation Policy Optimization (SDPO), which converts tokenized feedback into a dense learning signal without any external teacher or explicit reward model. SDPO treats the current model conditioned on feedback as a self-teacher and distills its feedback-informed next-token predictions back into the policy. In this way, SDPO leverages the model's ability to retrospectively identify its own mistakes in-context. Across scientific reasoning, tool use, and competitive programming on LiveCodeBench v6, SDPO improves sample efficiency and final accuracy over strong RLVR baselines. Notably, SDPO also outperforms baselines in standard RLVR environments that only return scalar feedback by using successful rollouts as implicit feedback for failed attempts. Finally, applying SDPO to individual questions at test time accelerates discovery on difficult binary-reward tasks, achieving the same discovery probability as best-of-k sampling or multi-turn conversations with 3x fewer attempts.",

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Get the RL via Self-Distillation paper (2601.20802) - very relevant
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2601.20802"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'

echo "---"
# Get the self-distillation enables continual learning paper (2601.19897)
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2601.19897"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'

echo "---"
# Get PRIME (Process Reinforcement through Implicit Rewards) abstract
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2502.01456"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'
raw_arguments.description: Get RL via Self-Distillation, continual learning, and PRIME papers

steps.reasoning_content:
I found a very important direct competitor: "Reinforcement Learning via Self-Distillation" (SDPO, 2601.20802). This paper is EXTREMELY similar to OpenClaw-RL's OPD method:
- OpenClaw-RL OPD: uses the current model conditioned on feedback (hints extracted from next state) to generate a teacher distribution, then distills back to the student
- SDPO (2601.20802): "treats the current model conditioned on feedback as a self-teacher and distills its feedback-informed next-token predictions back into the policy"


These are essentially the same technique applied to different domains—OpenClaw-RL leverages live next-state signals in agentic settings while SDPO uses textual feedback like runtime errors and judge evaluations in code and math tasks. This is a critical finding because SDPO (published January 2026) predates OpenClaw-RL (March 2026), meaning the core OPD method appears to have been independently proposed earlier. Let me check the SDPO abstract more carefully to understand how it converts tokenized feedback into dense learning signals without external teachers or reward models.

Both approaches use the same fundamental mechanism: they condition the model on feedback to generate a different token distribution that "knows" what the correct response should be, then use the per-token divergence between this informed distribution and the original policy as a learning signal. The key difference is really just the application domain—OpenClaw-RL applies this to live conversational interactions while SDPO focuses on code and math problems.

I'm also noticing that the privileged information distillation paper introduces OPSD (On-Policy Self-Distillation), which appears to be yet another instantiation of this same core idea—training a teacher conditioned on privileged information alongside an unconditioned student using either a joint objective or RL with KL penalties. This suggests there's a broader pattern here that multiple papers are converging on independently, though the OpenClaw-RL paper's failure to cite SDPO seems like a notable gap given how directly related they are.

What actually distinguishes OpenClaw-RL, then, is less about the OPD mechanism itself and more about how it's deployed: the application to live online learning from user interactions, the specific framing of next-state signals as a unified privileged information source across different agent types, the particular hint extraction mechanism for handling noisy signals, and the integration of Binary RL with OPD in an asynchronous production system. The core distillation method isn't novel, but the system design and application context might be.

I should verify whether the claim about being the first to unify multiple concurrent interaction streams holds up—Agent Lightning also tackles training arbitrary AI agents from diverse environments with decoupled training, though it's not specifically positioned as a personal-agent system. Let me search more thoroughly to see what else is out there.

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL."

# Now search for more concurrent work around self-distillation from feedback
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "SDPO self-distillation policy optimization rich feedback LLM RL 2026",
      "reinforcement learning rich textual feedback dense reward self-teacher",
      "on-policy self-distillation execution feedback code reasoning math 2026",
      "ExGRPO experience learning from execution 2026",
      "policy gradient token-level advantage dense feedback reward LLM"
    ],
    "max_results": 15,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -100
arguments.description: Search for SDPO concurrent self-distillation papers

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL."

# Now search for more concurrent work around self-distillation from feedback
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "SDPO self-distillation policy optimization rich feedback LLM RL 2026",
      "reinforcement learning rich textual feedback dense reward self-teacher",
      "on-policy self-distillation execution feedback code reasoning math 2026",
      "ExGRPO experience learning from execution 2026",
      "policy gradient token-level advantage dense feedback reward LLM"
    ],
    "max_results": 15,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -100
raw_arguments.description: Search for SDPO concurrent self-distillation papers

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Check ExGRPO
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2510.02245"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'

echo "---"
# Also check hubotter et al  - maybe there's an arxiv paper that corresponds to hubotter2026reinforcement citation
tvly search "hubotter reinforcement learning execution feedback distillation 2025 2026" --max-results 5

echo "==="
# And kleinebuening aligning
tvly search "kleinebuening aligning language model agent online next state 2026" --max-results 5
arguments.description: Find ExGRPO and Hubotter/Kleinebuening paper references

results.content:
"title": "ExGRPO: Learning to Reason from Experience",
        "abstract": "Reinforcement learning from verifiable rewards (RLVR) is an emerging paradigm for improving the reasoning ability of large language models. However, standard on-policy training discards rollout experiences after a single update, leading to computational inefficiency and instability. While prior work on RL has highlighted the benefits of reusing past experience, the role of experience characteristics in shaping learning dynamics of large reasoning models remains underexplored. In this paper, we are the first to investigate what makes a reasoning experience valuable and identify rollout correctness and entropy as effective indicators of experience value. Based on these insights, we propose ExGRPO (Experiential Group Relative Policy Optimization), a framework that organizes and prioritizes valuable experiences, and employs a mixed-policy objective to balance exploration with experience exploitation. Experiments on five backbone models (1.5B-8B parameters) show that ExGRPO consistently improves reasoning performance on mathematical/general benchmarks, with an average gain of +3.5/7.6 points over on-policy RLVR. Moreover, ExGRPO stabilizes training on both stronger and weaker models where on-policy methods fail. These results highlight principled experience management as a key ingredient for efficient and scalable RLVR.",
---

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Check ExGRPO
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2510.02245"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'

echo "---"
# Also check hubotter et al  - maybe there's an arxiv paper that corresponds to hubotter2026reinforcement citation
tvly search "hubotter reinforcement learning execution feedback distillation 2025 2026" --max-results 5

echo "==="
# And kleinebuening aligning
tvly search "kleinebuening aligning language model agent online next state 2026" --max-results 5
raw_arguments.description: Find ExGRPO and Hubotter/Kleinebuening paper references

steps.reasoning_content:
This confirms that "hubotter2026reinforcement" in the paper refers to SDPO (Reinforcement Learning via Self-Distillation). So the OpenClaw-RL paper IS citing the SDPO paper (as "hubotter2026reinforcement").

Let me re-read the paper carefully to verify:

From the paper's introduction:
"On-policy distillation methods~\citep{agarwal2024policy, shenfeld2026self, hubotter2026reinforcement} train LLMs on their own generations conditioned on execution feedback, achieving acceleration over GRPO but requiring pre-collected feedback-response pairs."


And: "In concurrent work, \citet{kleinebuening2026aligning} improves online policy by directly prompting with next-state information, but the corrective hints remain implicit."

So I've identified that kleinebuening2026aligning corresponds to "Aligning Language Models from User Interactions" on ResearchGate. The OpenClaw-RL paper claims that SDPO and similar methods operate on fixed datasets, though SDPO itself appears to have some online components that might contradict this characterization.

Looking more closely at the technical details, both SDPO and OPD follow essentially the same pipeline: they extract textual feedback or hints, augment the prompt with this information, use the model conditioned on the augmented context as a teacher, and derive per-token training signals from log-probability differences. The distinction between them seems more subtle than the papers initially suggest.

The actual differences appear to be around deployment and application rather than core methodology. OpenClaw-RL operates in a live setting where signals come from real-time interactions, explicitly filters hints for actionability from potentially noisy user responses, and targets personal agents with conversational adaptation—whereas SDPO was framed around pre-collected data. But even that framing may be misleading since SDPO itself demonstrates online capability using successful rollouts as implicit feedback. So the real novelty boils down to the infrastructure for live deployment, the personalization angle for conversational agents, and the deliberate hint extraction and quality filtering step.

Now I'm looking for the kleinebuening2026aligning paper—the ResearchGate result about "Aligning Language Models from User Interactions" seems like it could be the right match.

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Find kleinebuening's "Aligning Language Models from User Interactions"
tvly extract "https://www.researchgate.net/publication/aligning-language-models-user-interactions" --query "aligning language models user interactions" 2>/dev/null || true

# Search for it directly
tvly search "\"Aligning Language Models from User Interactions\" kleinebuening 2026" --max-results 5 --depth advanced
arguments.description: Find Kleinebuening aligning language models paper

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Find kleinebuening's "Aligning Language Models from User Interactions"
tvly extract "https://www.researchgate.net/publication/aligning-language-models-user-interactions" --query "aligning language models user interactions" 2>/dev/null || true

# Search for it directly
tvly search "\"Aligning Language Models from User Interactions\" kleinebuening 2026" --max-results 5 --depth advanced
raw_arguments.description: Find Kleinebuening aligning language models paper

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Get the kleinebuening paper from arxiv
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2603.12273"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"|"published"'

echo "---"
# Also look for shenfeld2026self  
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/search" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"query": "shenfeld self-play distillation on-policy language 2025 2026", "max_results": 5, "sort_by": "date"}' \
  | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"published"' | head -30
arguments.description: Get Kleinebuening paper and find Shenfeld self-play paper

results.content:
"title": "Aligning Language Models from User Interactions",
        "abstract": "Multi-turn user interactions are among the most abundant data produced by language models, yet we lack effective methods to learn from them. While typically discarded, these interactions often contain useful information: follow-up user messages may indicate that a response was incorrect, failed to follow an instruction, or did not align with the user's preferences. Importantly, language models are already able to make use of this information in context. After observing a user's follow-up, the same model is often able to revise its behavior. We leverage this ability to propose a principled and scalable method for learning directly from user interactions through self-distillation. By conditioning the model on the user's follow-up message and comparing the resulting token distribution with the original policy, we obtain a target for updating the policy that captures how the model's behavior changes in hindsight. We then distill this hindsight distribution back into the current policy. Remarkably, we show that training on real-world user conversations from WildChat improves language models across standard alignment and instruction-following benchmarks, without regressing other capabilities. The same mechanism enables personalization, allowing models to continually adapt to individual users through interaction without explicit feedback. Our results demonstrate that raw user interactions that arise naturally during deployment enable alignment, personalization, and continual adaptation.",
        "published": "2026-02-18 00:00:00",
---

steps.reasoning_content:
Excellent! Now I found the kleinebuening2026aligning paper (arxiv 2603.12273, "Aligning Language Models from User Interactions"). This is a CRITICAL finding. Let me compare its approach to OpenClaw-RL's OPD:

Kleinebuening2026aligning (arxiv 2603.12273, published February 18, 2026):
"By conditioning the model on the user's follow-up message and comparing the resulting token distribution with the original policy, we obtain a target for updating the policy that captures how the model's behavior changes in hindsight. We then distill this hindsight distribution back into the current policy."


OpenClaw-RL OPD (published March 10, 2026):
"if we augment the original prompt with a textual hint extracted from s_{t+1}, the same model produces a different token distribution... The per-token gap between this hint-enhanced distribution and the student distribution provides a directional advantage"

These are fundamentally the same approach—both use a follow-up signal to generate a target distribution for policy improvement—but OpenClaw-RL refines the signal by extracting a concise, filtered hint rather than using the raw user message. Kleinebuening also applies this same principle across multiple dimensions, which suggests this is a core technique that both teams independently converged on.

Kleinebuening demonstrates the method works on real conversations from WildChat, showing personalization and continual adaptation without explicit feedback signals, which directly overlaps with OpenClaw-RL's claims for the personal agent track. While OpenClaw-RL acknowledges this as concurrent work, the distinction is overstated—Kleinebuening's approach of using raw follow-ups is arguably more general than requiring explicit hint extraction, and both papers were published within weeks of each other. The real novelty in OpenClaw-RL lies in extending the method beyond conversational agents to terminal, GUI, and tool-use domains, plus the specific hint extraction mechanism itself.

I'm also seeing how OpenClaw-RL sits at the intersection with SDPO (Hübotter et al.), which focuses on verifiable code and math settings—so OpenClaw-RL's contribution is bringing that executable environment approach into conversational and live deployment contexts, unified across multiple agent types. Let me check what's been done on process rewards specifically for conversation, and look into the SETA terminal dataset they mention.

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL."

# The kleinebuening paper (2603.12273) is very similar to OPD for personal agents
# Now need to assess how novel the general agent part is
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "SETA terminal agent shell execution training RL dataset 2025 2026",
      "OSWorld-Verified multimodal GUI agent RL training Qwen3VL",
      "AReal asynchronous RL large-scale language reasoning system",
      "process reward model step-wise agent RL long-horizon terminal GUI SWE 2025",
      "unified multi-environment agent RL framework scalable cloud parallel"
    ],
    "max_results": 15,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -100
