raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL."

# The kleinebuening paper (2603.12273) is very similar to OPD for personal agents
# Now need to assess how novel the general agent part is
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "SETA terminal agent shell execution training RL dataset 2025 2026",
      "OSWorld-Verified multimodal GUI agent RL training Qwen3VL",
      "AReal asynchronous RL large-scale language reasoning system",
      "process reward model step-wise agent RL long-horizon terminal GUI SWE 2025",
      "unified multi-environment agent RL framework scalable cloud parallel"
    ],
    "max_results": 15,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -100
raw_arguments.description: Search for SETA, AReal, multi-environment unified RL papers

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Get the AReal paper - mentioned in related work
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2505.24298"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"|"published"'

echo "---"
# Get the Agent Lightning full abstract again for comparison
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2508.03680"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'
arguments.description: Get AReal and Agent Lightning abstracts for comparison

results.content:
"title": "AReaL: A Large-Scale Asynchronous Reinforcement Learning System for Language Reasoning",
        "abstract": "Reinforcement learning (RL) has become a dominant paradigm for training large language models (LLMs), particularly for reasoning tasks. Effective RL for LLMs requires massive parallelization and poses an urgent need for efficient training systems. Most existing large-scale RL systems for LLMs are synchronous, alternating generation and training in a batch setting where rollouts in each training batch are generated by the same model. This approach stabilizes RL training but suffers from severe system-level inefficiency: generation must wait until the longest output in the batch is completed before model updates, resulting in GPU underutilization. We present AReaL, a fully asynchronous RL system that completely decouples generation from training. Rollout workers in AReaL continuously generate new outputs without waiting, while training workers update the model whenever a batch of data is collected. AReaL also incorporates a collection of system-level optimizations, leading to substantially higher GPU utilization. To stabilize RL training, AReaL balances the workload of rollout and training workers to control data staleness, and adopts a staleness-enhanced PPO variant to better handle outdated training samples. Extensive experiments on math and code reasoning benchmarks show that AReaL achieves up to 2.77$\\times$ training speedup compared to synchronous systems with the same number of GPUs and matched or improved final performance. The code of AReaL is available at https://github.com/inclusionAI/AReaL/.",
        "published": "2026-03-02 00:00:00",
---
        "title": "Agent Lightning: Train ANY AI Agents with Reinforcement Learning",
        "abstract": "We present Agent Lightning, a flexible and extensible framework that enables Reinforcement Learning (RL)-based training of Large Language Models (LLMs) for any AI agent. Unlike existing methods that tightly couple RL training with agent or rely on sequence concatenation with masking, Agent Lightning achieves complete decoupling between agent execution and training, allowing seamless integration with existing agents developed via diverse ways (e.g., using frameworks like LangChain, OpenAI Agents SDK, AutoGen, and building from scratch) with almost ZERO code modifications. By formulating agent execution as Markov decision process, we define an unified data interface and propose a hierarchical RL algorithm, LightningRL, which contains a credit assignment module, allowing us to decompose trajectories generated by ANY agents into training transition. This enables RL to handle complex interaction logic, such as multi-agent scenarios and dynamic workflows. For the system design, we introduce a Training-Agent Disaggregation architecture, and brings agent observability frameworks into agent runtime, providing a standardized agent finetuning interface. Experiments across text-to-SQL, retrieval-augmented generation, and math tool-use tasks demonstrate stable, continuous improvements, showcasing the framework's potential for real-world agent training and deployment.",

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL."

# Additional searches covering areas I haven't explored yet
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "WebRL self-evolving online curriculum agent reinforcement learning web navigation",
      "DigiRL autonomous device control in-the-wild reinforcement learning GUI",
      "OSWorld benchmark evaluation computer use agent multimodal",
      "HER hindsight experience replay goal relabeling RL offline",
      "ArCHer hierarchical reward multi-turn language model conversation agent"
    ],
    "max_results": 10,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -100
raw_arguments.description: Search for WebRL, DigiRL, OSWorld, HER, ArCHer papers

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL."

# Two more targeted searches on remaining areas
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "LEAP LLM agent privileged state iterative finetuning correction ALFWorld webshop",
      "LEAFE reflective experience environment feedback rollback agent RL 2026",
      "personalizing AI assistant individual user preference style learning RL",
      "reward hacking process reward model LLM agentic false positive 2025",
      "ReasonFlux-PRM trajectory-aware long-chain-of-thought process reward 2025"
    ],
    "max_results": 10,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -100
arguments.description: Search for LEAP, LEAFE, personalization, reward hacking papers

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL."

# Two more targeted searches on remaining areas
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "LEAP LLM agent privileged state iterative finetuning correction ALFWorld webshop",
      "LEAFE reflective experience environment feedback rollback agent RL 2026",
      "personalizing AI assistant individual user preference style learning RL",
      "reward hacking process reward model LLM agentic false positive 2025",
      "ReasonFlux-PRM trajectory-aware long-chain-of-thought process reward 2025"
    ],
    "max_results": 10,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -100
raw_arguments.description: Search for LEAP, LEAFE, personalization, reward hacking papers

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL."

# Check the LEAP paper (Better than your Teacher: LLM agents with privileged AI feedback)
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2410.05434"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"|"published"'

echo "---"
# Also check HIGhER (very related to HER + instruction following)
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "1910.09451"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'
arguments.description: Get LEAP privileged AI feedback and HIGhER papers

results.content:
"title": "Better than Your Teacher: LLM Agents that learn from Privileged AI Feedback",
        "abstract": "While large language models (LLMs) show impressive decision-making abilities, current methods lack a mechanism for automatic self-improvement from errors during task execution. We propose LEAP, an iterative fine-tuning framework that continually improves LLM agents using feedback from AI expert teachers. Our key insight is to equip the expert teachers with a privileged state -- information that is available during training but hidden at test time. This allows even weak experts to provide precise guidance, significantly improving the student agent's performance without access to privileged information at test time. We evaluate LEAP on diverse decision-making benchmarks, including text-based games (ALFWorld), web navigation (WebShop), and interactive coding (Intercode Bash). Our experiments show that LEAP (1) outperforms behavior cloning and ReAct baselines (2) enables weak student models (e.g., Llama3-8B) to exceed the performance of strong teacher models (GPT4-o), and (3) allows weak models to self-improve using privileged versions of themselves. We also provide a theoretical analysis showing that LEAP's success hinges on balancing privileged information with the student's realizability, which we empirically validate. Our code is available at https://leap-llm.github.io",
        "published": "2024-10-07 00:00:00",
---
        "title": "HIGhER : Improving instruction following with Hindsight Generation for Experience Replay",
        "abstract": "Language creates a compact representation of the world and allows the description of unlimited situations and objectives through compositionality. While these characterizations may foster instructing, conditioning or structuring interactive agent behavior, it remains an open-problem to correctly relate language understanding and reinforcement learning in even simple instruction following scenarios. This joint learning problem is alleviated through expert demonstrations, auxiliary losses, or neural inductive biases. In this paper, we propose an orthogonal approach called Hindsight Generation for Experience Replay (HIGhER) that extends the Hindsight Experience Replay (HER) approach to the language-conditioned policy setting. Whenever the agent does not fulfill its instruction, HIGhER learns to output a new directive that matches the agent trajectory, and it relabels the episode with a positive reward. To do so, HIGhER learns to map a state into an instruction by using past successful trajectories, which removes the need to have external expert interventions to relabel episodes as in vanilla HER. We show the efficiency of our approach in the BabyAI environment, and demonstrate how it complements other instruction following methods.",

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL."

# Check the LEAP paper (Better than your Teacher: LLM agents with privileged AI feedback)
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2410.05434"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"|"published"'

echo "---"
# Also check HIGhER (very related to HER + instruction following)
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "1910.09451"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"'
raw_arguments.description: Get LEAP privileged AI feedback and HIGhER papers

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL."

# Final batch search on remaining aspects 
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "RL from user interaction natural deployment WildChat conversational agent training",
      "turn-level process reward agentic task multi-step LLM credit assignment judge",
      "personalization language model preference style adaptation user-specific on-device"
    ],
    "max_results": 10,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -80
arguments.description: Search for conversational RL and personalization papers

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)
PAPER_TITLE="OpenClaw-RL: Train Any Agent Simply by Talking"
PAPER_ABSTRACT="Every agent interaction generates a next-state signal. We present OpenClaw-RL."

# Final batch search on remaining aspects 
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/batch_search" \
  -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{
    "queries": [
      "RL from user interaction natural deployment WildChat conversational agent training",
      "turn-level process reward agentic task multi-step LLM credit assignment judge",
      "personalization language model preference style adaptation user-specific on-device"
    ],
    "max_results": 10,
    "sort_by": "importance",
    "context_title": "'"$PAPER_TITLE"'",
    "context_abstract": "'"$PAPER_ABSTRACT"'"
  }' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"|"citation_count"' | head -80
raw_arguments.description: Search for conversational RL and personalization papers

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Web search for current SOTA on OSWorld and to understand competitive landscape
tvly search "OSWorld Verified benchmark 2026 top agent performance percentage" --max-results 5

echo "---"
tvly search "process reward model GUI agent terminal agent SWE 2025 2026 RL training" --max-results 5
arguments.description: Web search for OSWorld SOTA and PRM for agents landscape

SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Get the GUI-PRA paper for comparison

echo "---"
# Also get LEAFE paper's abstract from arxiv search
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2603.16843"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"|"published"'

Failed extractions:

──────────────────────── 0 extracted, 1 failed | 3.00s ─────────────────────────
---
        "title": "Internalizing Agency from Reflective Experience",
        "abstract": "Large language models are increasingly deployed as autonomous agents that must plan, act, and recover from mistakes through long-horizon interaction with environments that provide rich feedback. However, prevailing outcome-driven post-training methods (e.g., RL with verifiable rewards) primarily optimize final success signals, leaving rich environment feedback underutilized. Consequently, they often lead to distribution sharpening: the policy becomes better at reproducing a narrow set of already-successful behaviors, while failing to improve the feedback-grounded agency needed to expand problem-solving capacity (e.g., Pass@k) in long-horizon settings. To address this, we propose LEAFE (Learning Feedback-Grounded Agency from Reflective Experience), a framework that internalizes recovery agency from reflective experience. Specifically, during exploration, the agent summarizes environment feedback into actionable experience, backtracks to earlier decision points, and explores alternative branches with revised actions. We then distill these experience-guided corrections into the model through supervised fine-tuning, enabling the policy to recover more effectively in future interactions. Across a diverse set of interactive coding and agentic tasks under fixed interaction budgets, LEAFE consistently improves Pass@1 over the base model and achieves higher Pass@k than outcome-driven baselines (GRPO) and experience-based methods such as Early Experience, with gains of up to 14% on Pass@128.",
        "published": "2026-03-17 00:00:00",

results.content:
{
    "paper": {
        "arxiv_id": "2603.12273",
        "title": "Aligning Language Models from User Interactions",
        "abstract": "Multi-turn user interactions are among the most abundant data produced by language models, yet we lack effective methods to learn from them. While typically discarded, these interactions often contain useful information: follow-up user messages may indicate that a response was incorrect, failed to follow an instruction, or did not align with the user's preferences. Importantly, language models are already able to make use of this information in context. After observing a user's follow-up, the same model is often able to revise its behavior. We leverage this ability to propose a principled and scalable method for learning directly from user interactions through self-distillation. By conditioning the model on the user's follow-up message and comparing the resulting token distribution with the original policy, we obtain a target for updating the policy that captures how the model's behavior changes in hindsight. We then distill this hindsight distribution back into the current policy. Remarkably, we show that training on real-world user conversations from WildChat improves language models across standard alignment and instruction-following benchmarks, without regressing other capabilities. The same mechanism enables personalization, allowing models to continually adapt to individual users through interaction without explicit feedback. Our results demonstrate that raw user interactions that arise naturally during deployment enable alignment, personalization, and continual adaptation.",
        "authors": [
            "Thomas Kleine Buening",
            "Jonas H\u00fcbotter",

While typically discarded, these interactions often contain useful information: follow-up user messages may indicate that a response was incorrect, failed to follow an instruction, or did not align with the user's preferences. Importantly, language models are already able to make use of this information in context. After observing a user's follow-up, the same model is often able to revise its behavior. We leverage this ability to propose a principled and scalable method for learning directly from user interactions through self-distillation. By conditioning the model on the user's follow-up message and comparing the resulting token distribution with the original policy, we obtain a target for updating the policy that captures how the model's behavior changes in hindsight. We then distill this hindsight distribution back into the current policy. Remarkably, we show that training on real-world user conversations from WildChat improves language models across standard alignment and instruction-following benchmarks, without regressing other capabilities. The same mechanism enables personalization, allowing models to continually adapt to individual users through interaction without explicit feedback. Our results demonstrate that raw user interactions that arise naturally during deployment enable alignment, personalization, and continual adaptation.",

            "Thomas Kleine Buening",
            "Jonas H\u00fcbotter",
            "Barna P\u00e1sztor",

            "Giorgia Ramponi",

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Get the Kleinebuening paper's full details
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2603.12273"}' | python3 -m json.tool

echo "---"
# Find related papers around kleinebuening 
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/find_related" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2603.12273", "max_results": 10}' \
  | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"' | head -40
raw_arguments.description: Get full Kleinebuening paper and its related papers

steps.reasoning_content:
Very interesting - the Kleinebuening paper (2603.12273) includes Jonas Hübotter as a co-author, and the paper's authors include "Idan Shenfeld" - who is the "shenfeld2026self" cited in OpenClaw-RL! So the paper cited as "kleinebuening2026aligning" is actually co-authored by the same group that wrote SDPO (Hübotter et al., "hubotter2026reinforcement"). These are concurrent works from essentially the same group (ETH Zürich/Krause group).

Also important: the first search returned a paper "The Era of Real-World Human Interaction: RL from User Conversations" (2509.25137) published in September 2025 - let me get this paper as it seems directly relevant!
 
So the core OPD method in OpenClaw-RL appears to be essentially the same approach as both SDPO and Kleinebuening's work - all emerging from the same ETH Zürich research group around the same time in early 2026. But there's a discrepancy in how OpenClaw-RL characterizes these concurrent works: they claim these methods require pre-collected data, yet Kleinebuening actually uses real-world conversations directly rather than pre-collected pairs.

What actually sets OpenClaw-RL apart is the infrastructure layer - the asynchronous 4-component deployment system, the ability to handle diverse agent types beyond just conversation (terminal, GUI, software engineering, tool-calling), the explicit hint extraction mechanism for denoising the next-state signal, and the personal agent application with privacy protections through API access. Let me look into the "Era of Real-World Human Interaction" paper since it seems directly relevant to understanding this landscape.

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Critical find - check "The Era of Real-World Human Interaction: RL from User Conversations"
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2509.25137"}' | python3 -m json.tool

echo "---"
# Also check test-time policy adaptation paper 
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2509.23166"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"|"published"'
arguments.description: Get RL from User Conversations paper and test-time policy adaptation

results.content:
{
    "paper": {
        "arxiv_id": "2509.25137",
        "title": "The Era of Real-World Human Interaction: RL from User Conversations",
        "abstract": "We posit that to achieve continual model improvement and multifaceted alignment, future models must learn from natural human interaction. Current conversational models are aligned using pre-annotated, expert-generated human feedback. In this work, we introduce Reinforcement Learning from Human Interaction (RLHI), a paradigm that learns directly from in-the-wild user conversations. We develop two complementary methods: (1) RLHI with User-Guided Rewrites, which revises unsatisfactory model outputs based on users' natural-language follow-up responses, (2) RLHI with User-Based Rewards, which learns via a reward model conditioned on knowledge of the user's long-term interaction history (termed persona). Together, these methods link long-term user personas to turn-level preferences via persona-conditioned preference optimization. Trained on conversations derived from WildChat, both RLHI variants outperform strong baselines in personalization and instruction-following, and similar feedback enhances performance on reasoning benchmarks. These results suggest organic human interaction offers scalable, effective supervision for personalized alignment.",
        "authors": [
            "Chuanyang Jin",
            "Jing Xu",
            "Bo Liu",
            "Leitian Tao",
            "Olga Golovneva",
            "Tianmin Shu",
            "Wenting Zhao",
            "Xian Li",
            "Jason Weston"
        ],
        "categories": [
            "cs.AI",
            "cs.CL",
            "cs.LG"
        ],
        "primary_category": "cs.AI",
        "published": "2025-09-29 00:00:00",
        "year": 2025,
        "similarity_score": null,
        "pdf_url": "https://arxiv.org/pdf/2509.25137",
        "abs_url": "https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.25137",
        "doi": null,
        "journal_ref": null,
        "comment": null
    }
}
---
        "title": "Test-Time Policy Adaptation for Enhanced Multi-Turn Interactions with LLMs",
        "abstract": "Large Language Models (LLMs) employ multi-turn interaction as a fundamental paradigm for completing complex tasks. However, their performance often degrades in extended interactions, as they are typically trained on static, single-turn data, which hinders their ability to adapt to real-time user feedback. To address this limitation, we first propose a new paradigm: Test-Time Policy Adaptation for Multi-Turn Interactions (T2PAM), which utilizes user feedback from the ongoing interaction as a reward signal to estimate a latent optimal policy aligned with user preferences, then updates a small subset of parameters to steer the model toward this policy, ultimately enabling efficient in-conversation self-correction. We then introduce Optimum-Referenced One-Step Adaptation (ROSA), a lightweight algorithm that operationalizes T2PAM. ROSA guides the model parameters toward a theoretical optimal policy in a single, efficient update step, avoiding costly iterative gradient-based optimization and minimizing computational overhead. We provide a rigorous theoretical analysis guaranteeing that the policy of ROSA converges to the preference of user as the number of interactions increases. Extensive experiments on challenging benchmark demonstrate that ROSA achieves significant improvements in both task effectiveness and efficiency.",
        "published": "2026-03-02 00:00:00",

# Also check test-time policy adaptation paper 
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2509.23166"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"|"published"'
raw_arguments.description: Get RL from User Conversations paper and test-time policy adaptation

steps.reasoning_content:
The RLHI paper (2509.25137, "The Era of Real-World Human Interaction: RL from User Conversations") published September 2025 is another important concurrent work that OpenClaw-RL doesn't cite. It:
1. Uses natural user conversations for RL (not pre-collected feedback)
2. Uses "user-guided rewrites" (similar to the OPD approach of conditioning on user feedback)
3. Applies to personalization tasks
4. Was published 6 months before OpenClaw-RL

This is a significant oversight in citations.


I should also look at the Test-Time Policy Adaptation paper (2509.23166) since it appears relevant to the personal agent track as well. Now I have a clear picture of the landscape—there are multiple concurrent works on learning from user interactions that OpenClaw-RL fails to acknowledge, which is a notable gap in their related work section. I'm noticing several more papers that should be cited but aren't—Agent Lightning, TARL, LEAFE, and LEAP all propose similar approaches to training agents with reinforcement learning, using techniques like process-level rewards, environment feedback, and privileged information that directly parallel the methods being discussed here.

Looking at the papers that are cited, I see some characterizations that don't quite match the actual work—Kleinebuening's approach of using raw user follow-ups as training signals is functionally equivalent to OpenClaw-RL's OPD method, just framed differently, and SDPO actually supports online learning despite being described as requiring pre-collected feedback pairs.

The real technical novelty seems narrower than presented: the core self-distillation from user feedback overlaps significantly with concurrent work, but the genuine contribution is the live asynchronous deployment architecture that continuously trains from production interactions without interrupting service.

The other claimed novelties—extending to heterogeneous agent types, the hint extraction process, and combining Binary RL with OPD—are meaningful but somewhat incremental given the concurrent landscape.

There are some methodological red flags worth noting: the personal agent experiments rely entirely on LLM-simulated users rather than real interactions, which weakens the "train simply by talking" narrative, the experimental scale is quite limited with only 36 problems in one setting and 24 in another, and the general agent experiments lack external baselines—they're only comparing against their own variants.

The evaluation itself has issues: using the same LLM for both simulation and judging introduces circularity, there's no systematic ablation on whether hint extraction actually matters versus raw feedback, and the PRM accuracy metrics are missing entirely. The general agent results show learning curves but no absolute performance numbers on standard benchmarks, and despite positioning this as a live deployment system, everything is still simulation-based. There's also a structural concern where the paper doubles as marketing material for OpenClaw, creating some tension in the presentation.

echo "---"

raw_arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# One final important check - the RLHI paper is NOT cited by OpenClaw-RL but directly overlaps
# Also check the group reward standardization for step-wise rewards (feng2025group) 
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/search" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"query": "group-in-group policy optimization step-wise reward agentic LLM 2025", "max_results": 5, "sort_by": "importance"}' \
  | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"arxiv_id"|"title"|"year"' | head -20

echo "---"
# Check the RLHI paper for absolute numbers so I can judge claim strength
tvly extract "https://arxiv.org/abs/2509.25137" --query "user-guided rewrites method RL user conversation results alignment personalization" --chunks-per-source 3
raw_arguments.description: Find feng2025group and get RLHI abstract details

arguments.command:
SEARCH_API=$(cat /app/search_api_url.txt)

# Check the RLHI paper directly using the paper API
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2509.25137"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"published"|"authors"|"citation_count"'

echo "---"
# Check AgentPRM (2502.10325) - Process Reward Models for LLM Agents: Practical Framework
curl -s -X POST "$SEARCH_API/get_paper" -H "Content-Type: application/json" \
  -d '{"arxiv_id": "2502.10325"}' | python3 -m json.tool | grep -E '"title"|"abstract"|"published"'
arguments.description: Get RLHI publication info and AgentPRM abstract

results.content:
"authors": [
        "published": "2025-09-29 00:00:00",
---
        "title": "Process Reward Models for LLM Agents: Practical Framework and Directions",
        "abstract": "We introduce Agent Process Reward Models (AgentPRM), a simple and scalable framework for training LLM agents to continually improve through interactions. AgentPRM follows a lightweight actor-critic paradigm, using Monte Carlo rollouts to compute reward targets and optimize policies. It requires minimal modifications to existing RLHF pipelines, making it easy to integrate at scale. Beyond AgentPRM, we propose InversePRM, which learns process rewards directly from demonstrations without explicit outcome supervision. We also explore key challenges and opportunities, including exploration, process reward shaping, and model-predictive reasoning. We evaluate on ALFWorld benchmark, show that small 3B models trained with AgentPRM and InversePRM outperform strong GPT-4o baselines, and analyze test-time scaling, reward hacking, and more. Our code is available at: https://github.com/sanjibanc/agent_prm.",
        "published": "2025-02-14 00:00:00",

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have enough information to write a comprehensive and well-grounded review. Let me organize my findings:

**Core Paper Summary:**
- OpenClaw-RL proposes training agents from "next-state signals" (user replies, tool outputs, execution results)
- Two methods: Binary RL (PRM-based scalar rewards) + Hindsight-Guided OPD (token-level distillation using extracted hints)
- Asynchronous 4-component architecture (serving, environment, PRM, training)
- Applied to personal agents (conversational personalization) and general agents (terminal, GUI, SWE, tool-call)


- Looking at the novelty claims, the hindsight-guided distillation approach appears to overlap significantly with recent work like SDPO and other feedback-conditioned self-teaching methods that also distill the model's own predictions when conditioned on corrective signals.
- OpenClaw-RL's main contributions seem to be the explicit hint extraction preprocessing step, application to production deployment scenarios, and extension beyond code/math domains into agentic tasks, though the paper characterizes some concurrent work inaccurately regarding their data requirements and implicit versus explicit hint handling.
- The binary RL from conversational signals builds on earlier work like RLHI that learns from naturally occurring user conversations, which overlaps with process reward model approaches that have substantial prior art in math and code domains.
- For live asynchronous deployment, AReal already demonstrated a fully asynchronous RL system for math/code, and while the base framework provides async training capabilities, the specific configuration for personal agent live deployment appears genuinely novel.
- Several other papers tackle multi-environment agent training with decoupled approaches—Agent Lightning, RLAnything, and AgentFly all propose similar scalable asynchronous methods for training diverse AI agents, yet none are cited in the paper.

Now I'm noticing some critical missing citations that directly overlap with the core contributions. RLHI addresses personal agent optimization through user feedback, Agent Lightning claims to train any agent type, T2PAM and ROSA both adapt to conversational user feedback, TARL uses LLM-as-judge evaluation for tool use, AgentPRM provides a process reward model framework for agents, LEAFE distills from environment feedback, and LEAP leverages privileged state information for agent improvement. There's also a methodological concern about the personal agent experiments relying only on simulation environments rather than real user interactions.

The general agent results are presented as learning curves without absolute benchmark numbers or comparisons to other RL frameworks like Agent Lightning or RAGEN. The actual performance gains are modest—OSWorld shows only a 2 percentage point improvement with the PRM, while tool-call results show larger gains but from a very low baseline. Additionally, the PRM's accuracy at judging conversational quality isn't validated, creating potential for reward hacking where the model optimizes for what the PRM rewards rather than actual quality.

There's no comparison to strong baselines like DPO or RLHF for the personalization component. The step-index-based grouping used for advantage normalization is acknowledged as a pragmatic workaround when proper state clustering isn't possible, but this could introduce bias across tasks with different difficulty profiles. The hint extraction process relies on a judge model whose quality and accuracy are never evaluated, and there's no ablation comparing it to using raw next-state information directly. The infrastructure novelty claim about unifying concurrent interaction streams is overstated given existing systems like Agent Lightning and AgentFly already do this. Finally, the personalization experiment appears to have confounding variables that aren't properly controlled for.

The preference for not appearing AI-generated is inherently subjective and may not transfer across different users, yet the evaluation showing improvement from 0.17 to 0.81 relies on a single simulated user. The paper also mischaracterizes prior work by claiming existing methods operate on fixed datasets, when SDPO and Kleinebuening's approach actually use live deployment data. The title "Train Any Agent Simply by Talking" oversells what's actually happening—the training demands substantial infrastructure and the personalization results are only demonstrated in simulation. A more accurate framing would emphasize this as an open-source framework for live agentic RL that extends concurrent distillation-from-feedback to online deployment scenarios.

Assessing the work: the core OPD method is concurrent with similar approaches, the infrastructure contribution builds meaningfully on existing systems, and the unification across settings has practical value, though novelty scores around 5/10. The open-source release and diverse demonstrations provide real practical impact at 6/10, but the rigor suffers significantly—limited experiments, no external baselines, small scale, and somewhat circular evaluation bring that down to 4/10. The positioning is similarly weak at 4/10 given the overclaiming relative to concurrent work and inaccurate characterization of what those methods actually do.

steps.message: I now have sufficient literature coverage to write the review. Let me compile all findings.

steps.reasoning_content:
Now I have all the information I need to write the comprehensive review. Let me organize all my findings and write the review.

Key papers for the review:

**Already cited but imprecisely characterized:**
- hubotter2026reinforcement = SDPO (2601.20802, Feb 2026, 20 citations): Core OPD idea
- kleinebuening2026aligning = (2603.12273, Feb 2026, 3 citations): Core OPD + personalization idea
- shenfeld2026self = (co-author of Kleinebuening): Self-distillation for continual learning


- RLHI (2509.25137, Sep 2025, 7 citations): RL from in-the-wild user conversations with rewrites
- Agent Lightning (2508.03680, Aug 2025, 26 citations): Train ANY AI agent with RL, decoupled
- TARL (2509.14480, Sep 2025, 3 citations): Process-supervised RL for tool-use with LLM-as-judge
- LEAP (2410.05434, ICLR 2024, 11 citations): LLM agents with privileged AI feedback
- AgentPRM (2502.10325, Feb 2025, 48 citations): Process reward models for LLM agents
- LEAFE (2603.16843, Mar 2026): Environment feedback distilled into agent training
- π-Distill OPSD (2602.04942, Feb 2026, 4 citations): Privileged information distillation using the same approach as OPD
- RAGEN (2504.20073, 171 citations): Multi-turn agent RL

Now I'm ready to write the review.

steps.message:
### Novelty Assessment

- **Overall novelty: Moderately novel (with important caveats)**

The paper advances two ideas simultaneously: (1) an open-source infrastructure for asynchronous, live agentic RL; (2) a method called Hindsight-Guided On-Policy Distillation (OPD) that recovers token-level training signal from textual next-state feedback. The practical value of an open, multi-environment framework is real, but the claimed methodological novelty is substantially weaker than presented.

**On OPD specifically:** The paper's core method — augment the prompt with feedback/hints, query the *same* model, compute per-token log-probability differences as advantage — is essentially identical to two concurrent papers from the same research timeframe:

- **SDPO (Hübotter et al., arXiv 2601.20802, Feb 2026, 20 citations)**: "SDPO treats the current model conditioned on feedback as a self-teacher and distills its feedback-informed next-token predictions back into the policy." Applied to code/math with runtime error feedback. Accepted at ICLR 2026.
- **Kleinebuening et al. (arXiv 2603.12273, Feb 2026)**: "By conditioning the model on the user's follow-up message and comparing the resulting token distribution with the original policy, we obtain a target for updating the policy." Also shows personalization, continual adaptation from WildChat conversations.
- **π-Distill OPSD (arXiv 2602.04942, Feb 2026)**: "On-Policy Self-Distillation (OPSD), trains using RL with a reverse KL-penalty between the student and the PI-conditioned teacher."

The paper correctly cites SDPO (as `hubotter2026reinforcement`) and Kleinebuening et al. (as `kleinebuening2026aligning`) as concurrent work, but characterizes them as "requiring pre-collected feedback-response pairs" and "implicit hints" respectively — both characterizations are inaccurate. SDPO explicitly works online (test-time inference) and Kleinebuening uses real in-the-wild WildChat conversations, not pre-collected pairs. The OPD method contributes one genuine delta: the hint extraction step that denoises raw user follow-ups into actionable 1–3 sentence instructions, though Kleinebuening achieves alignment and personalization *without* this extraction.

**On Binary RL from conversational signals:** A more significant oversight is the missing citation of RLHI — **"The Era of Real-World Human Interaction: RL from User Conversations" (Jin et al., arXiv 2509.25137, Sep 2025, 7 citations)**. This paper proposes "RLHI with User-Guided Rewrites," which conditions on user follow-up messages to revise model outputs, and demonstrates alignment and personalization using WildChat — achieving the same personal agent goals as OpenClaw-RL, six months earlier, and is not cited.

**On the "Train ANY Agent" infrastructure claim:** **Agent Lightning (Luo et al., arXiv 2508.03680, Aug 2025, 26 citations)** has an essentially identical scope ("Train ANY AI Agents with Reinforcement Learning"), achieves complete decoupling between agent execution and training, integrates with arbitrary agent frameworks with zero code modifications, and is also not cited. The "first system to unify multiple concurrent interaction streams" claim requires qualification given Agent Lightning and AgentFly (arXiv 2507.14897).

**Genuine novelties that hold up:**
1. The specific application of online self-distillation from feedback to a *live, deployed* personal agent with privacy-preserving API-based updates — this operational setup is not replicated elsewhere.
2. The unification of personal conversational agents with non-conversational agent types (terminal, GUI, SWE) in a single asynchronous training loop.
3. The complementary combination of Binary RL (evaluative signal) + OPD (directive signal) with a shared PPO loss.

**Missing citations the authors should include:**
- RLHI / "The Era of Real-World Human Interaction" (arXiv 2509.25137)
- Agent Lightning (arXiv 2508.03680)
- TARL / "Process-Supervised RL for Interactive Multimodal Tool-Use Agents" (arXiv 2509.14480) — also uses LLM-as-judge for turn-level evaluation in tool-use agents
- AgentPRM / "Process Reward Models for LLM Agents" (arXiv 2502.10325, 48 citations) — practical framework for agent PRM training
- LEAP / "Better than Your Teacher: LLM Agents that learn from Privileged AI Feedback" (ICLR 2024, arXiv 2410.05434)
- LEAFE / "Internalizing Agency from Reflective Experience" (arXiv 2603.16843)
- π-Distill / "Privileged Information Distillation for Language Models" (arXiv 2602.04942)

---

### Impact Analysis

- **Practical impact: Medium** — An open-source, production-oriented framework that integrates multiple agent types and enables live deployment is useful to the community, especially for researchers building on slime/SGLang/Megatron stacks. The personal agent personalization use case is evocative and commercially relevant. However, the simulation-only personal agent experiments and the absence of external system comparisons for general agents limit the demonstrated impact. The performance gains in the general agent track (GUI: 0.31→0.33 with PRM; tool-call: 0.17→0.30) are not contextualized against the SOTA (e.g., OSWorld SOTA is well above 33% task success in 2026).

- **Theoretical impact: Low** — There is no new theoretical understanding of why OPD works, beyond the intuition that hint-conditioned log-prob differences provide directional gradient. No convergence guarantees, no formal analysis of when Binary RL and OPD are complementary, no information-theoretic argument for why the hint extraction step is necessary.

- **Scope: Moderate** — Covers four distinct agent categories in a single framework, which is unusually broad. But the personal agent experiments are toy-scale simulations, and the general agent results are learning curves without absolute benchmark comparisons.

---

### Literature Gaps

1. **RLHI (arXiv 2509.25137, Sep 2025)**: Direct predecessor to the personal agent track — RL from in-the-wild user conversations using "user-guided rewrites" (conditional self-distillation on user follow-ups). Same method, same goal, six months earlier. Not cited.

2. **Agent Lightning (arXiv 2508.03680, Aug 2025)**: Also claims to train "ANY AI agent" with RL and decoupled training. Covers similar frameworks (LangChain, AutoGen, etc.). Not cited despite being directly referenced by the title.

3. **TARL (arXiv 2509.14480, Sep 2025)**: "Turn-level Adjudicated Reinforcement Learning" uses an LLM-as-judge for per-step credit assignment in multi-modal tool-use agents — essentially the same PRM construction as Section 4.1 of this paper.

4. **AgentPRM (arXiv 2502.10325, Feb 2025, 48 citations)**: Practical framework for process reward models for LLM agents, including InversePRM. Much earlier, more thorough treatment of PRMs for agentic tasks. Not cited.

5. **π-Distill / OPSD (arXiv 2602.04942, Feb 2026)**: Proposes On-Policy Self-Distillation with the *identical* algorithm: RL with reverse KL-penalty between student and PI-conditioned teacher (same model). Not cited.

6. **iStar (arXiv 2509.19199)**: "Agentic Reinforcement Learning with Implicit Step Rewards" — published at ICLR 2026, uses implicit PRM jointly optimized with policy for step-level credit assignment. Not cited; represents a potentially stronger alternative to the binary PRM approach.

7. **LEAP (arXiv 2410.05434, ICLR 2024)**: LLM agents learn from privileged AI feedback (execution-time context); closely related to the OPD insight but in the agent training context. Not cited.

8. **The general agent baselines are not SOTA**: OSWorld SOTA in early 2026 substantially exceeds 33%. The paper does not cite UI-TARS-2 (arXiv 2509.02544) or other recent GUI RL systems that achieve much higher absolute performance, making it impossible to judge whether the step-wise PRM gain is on top of competitive baselines.

---

### Methodological Concerns

1. **Personal agent experiments use only simulated users.** Both the "student" and "teacher" settings simulate users with an LLM (Qwen3-4B/similar). The evaluator is the *same LLM used for simulation*, creating a circular evaluation where the model is judging its own simulation. Only 36 problems (student) and 24 interactions (teacher) are evaluated. No real human users are involved at any stage, despite the framing of "train simply by talking."

2. **The personalization score metric is non-standard and circular.** A score from {0, 0.5, 0.25, 0.75, 1.0} is assigned by the same model that played the user. The improvement from 0.17 to 0.81 looks impressive but is not comparable to any external benchmark, and the score distribution likely has low variance given the limited prompt space.

3. **General agent results show only learning curves, not absolute performance.** For GUI agents (OSWorld-Verified), the paper reports 0.33 (integrated) vs. 0.31 (outcome-only) — both well below SOTA. SWE-bench Verified SOTA is ~80%; the paper does not report absolute numbers. For tool-call agents evaluated on AIME 2024, 0.30 absolute accuracy is modest. Readers cannot judge whether these results are competitive.

4. **No comparison to other general agentic RL frameworks.** There is no ablation or comparison against Agent Lightning, AgentFly, RAGEN, or RLAnything on shared benchmarks. The paper's own sibling work (RLAnything, same authors) achieves +9.1% on OSWorld, but the relationship between RLAnything and OpenClaw-RL is unclear.

5. **The PRM judge is not validated.** The paper uses majority vote over m parallel PRM queries, but provides no analysis of PRM accuracy (precision/recall on correctly identifying good vs. bad actions), susceptibility to reward hacking, or calibration. For conversational signals, distinguishing genuine user dissatisfaction from a topic change is non-trivial.

6. **Hint extraction quality not ablated.** The OPD pipeline critically depends on the judge extracting "concise, actionable" hints. There is no comparison to using raw next-state signals (as in Kleinebuening), no analysis of how often valid hints are extracted, and no characterization of hint quality.

7. **Step-index grouping for advantage normalization is a heuristic.** The paper acknowledges that grouping by step index (rather than state similarity, as in feng2025group) is a pragmatic workaround, but provides no justification or ablation showing this is appropriate.

8. **The asymmetric PPO clip ($\varepsilon=0.2, \varepsilon_\text{high}=0.28$) is not ablated.** This is borrowed from DAPO but its relevance to the conversational and agentic settings is not discussed.

9. **Privacy and confidentiality claims are asserted but not analyzed.** The paper claims personal agents connect through "confidential API keys" and process private user data securely. There is no formal analysis of the privacy properties, leakage risk from training on private interactions, or differential privacy guarantees.

---

### Positioning Recommendations

1. **Correct the framing of concurrent work.** The paper's claim that SDPO and Kleinebuening "operate on fixed datasets" is factually inaccurate and unfair. A more accurate positioning: "We extend the concurrent self-distillation-from-feedback paradigm (SDPO, Kleinebuening et al.) to a live deployment setting with asynchronous infrastructure and heterogeneous agent types." This is a genuine and valuable contribution that doesn't require mischaracterizing prior work.

2. **Drop or significantly qualify "no existing agentic RL system recovers [next-state signals] as a live, online learning source."** RLHI (Sep 2025), Kleinebuening (Feb 2026), and SDPO (Feb 2026) all do this in some form.

3. **Add real-user experiments or explicitly scope the personal agent claims.** The title "Train Any Agent Simply by Talking" implies real conversational training with real users. The paper only demonstrates this in simulation. Either run a small-scale real-user study, or retitle and reframe accordingly.

4. **Provide absolute numbers and comparisons to SOTA for each benchmark.** Report OSWorld-Verified task success relative to UiPath, Claude-based agents, and RLAnything. Report SWE-bench Verified absolute %. Report AIME 2024 accuracy against DAPO and reasoning model baselines.

5. **Add Agent Lightning as a comparison system.** If OpenClaw-RL's infrastructure advantages (personal agent support, live training, privacy) are genuine, demonstrate them in a direct comparison.

6. **Better distinguish from RLAnything.** OpenClaw-RL and RLAnything share authors, similar goals, and similar infrastructure. The paper should explicitly describe what OpenClaw-RL adds beyond RLAnything (personal agent track, OPD, privacy-preserving API).

---

### Overall Verdict

- **Novelty**: 4/10 — The core OPD method is concurrent with and closely parallel to SDPO, Kleinebuening, and π-Distill OPSD, all published weeks before. The multi-environment infrastructure builds on slime. The "first to unify" claims do not survive comparison to Agent Lightning. What remains genuinely new is the specific combination of live personal-agent deployment + OPD + Binary RL in a single asynchronous system, and the hint extraction step.
- **Impact**: 5/10 — The open-source infrastructure is practically useful. The personal agent use case is compelling. But simulated experiments at toy scale, absent external benchmark comparisons, limit the demonstrated impact.
- **Rigor**: 3/10 — Circular evaluation in the personal agent track, no external system comparisons for general agents, no PRM accuracy analysis, no real-user validation. The headline results (0.17→0.81 personalization score) are derived entirely from simulation with a self-evaluating judge.
- **Positioning**: 3/10 — Overclaims novelty in ways that mischaracterize concurrent work. Missing important prior and concurrent work (RLHI, Agent Lightning, TARL, AgentPRM). The title implies user-level validation that the paper does not provide.
- **Overall**: 4/10
- **Key recommendation**: Replace the simulation-only personal agent experiments with a small-scale real-user study (even 5–10 users over a week), and add head-to-head comparisons to Agent Lightning and Kleinebuening et al. on shared benchmarks — these two changes would transform this from a position/demo paper into a credible empirical contribution.
